macro_events.event_summary: Sustained Iran war into 10th week has driven gasoline prices high enough that filling a Ford F-150 now costs ~$160, signaling persistent crude/refined product price elevation.
primary_drivers: Sustained crude oil price elevation from Middle East supply risk premium
Demand destruction at the pump shifting consumer behavior toward fuel efficiency
Refining margin (crack spread) expansion from tight product markets

beneficiaries.rationale: Integrated major with ~2.4M boe/d upstream production; every $10 sustained Brent move adds materially to upstream EBITDA, and downstream refining capture also expands with elevated cracks.
Direct upstream leverage to Brent with ~3M boe/d production; Permian and Gulf assets see immediate cash flow uplift at sustained $100+ crude.
High-beta US shale pure-play (Permian); higher leverage means amplified equity response to sustained crude prices, plus Buffett endorsement floor.
Largest US independent refiner; product cracks widen when retail gasoline stays elevated against feedstock, directly expanding margins per barrel.
Pure-play refiner with Gulf Coast complex capable of running discounted crudes; sustained $4+/gal retail gasoline implies expanded crack spreads.
10 weeks of $160 fill-ups is long enough to shift consumer purchase intent toward EVs; historically Tesla order flow correlates positively with gas price spikes lasting 4+ weeks.
Hybrid leader (Prius, RAV4 Hybrid, Camry Hybrid) — best-positioned ICE OEM for fuel-economy-driven buying shift, with available inventory unlike pure EV makers.
Oilfield services see capex unlock when E&Ps run sustained $90+ WTI; completion activity and pricing power return after 10 weeks of confirmed price floor.

losers.rationale: F-150 is THE vehicle cited in the headline — $160 fill-up directly threatens demand for Ford's highest-margin, highest-volume product line; ICE truck mix is ~60%+ of North America profit.
Silverado/Sierra full-size trucks face same fuel-cost demand destruction as F-150; truck/SUV mix dominates GM North America earnings.
Jet fuel is ~25-30% of operating costs; sustained crude at war-premium levels with limited hedge coverage compresses margins meaningfully into summer travel season.
Same jet fuel exposure as DAL; United historically runs lower hedge ratios, making it more sensitive to sustained crude elevation.
Southwest's domestic-only network means no fare-mix offset from premium international cabins; fuel cost passthrough is hardest for low-cost carriers.
Bunker fuel costs rise with crude; cruise lines also face discretionary spend pressure as consumers absorb $160 fill-ups for 10+ weeks.

Casual dining (Olive Garden, LongHorn) is squeezed by gas-price-driven discretionary income compression on middle-income consumers — historically high correlation between pump prices and casual dining traffic.
Lower-income core customer base sees direct wallet share loss to gasoline; sustained 10-week elevation forces trade-down within Walmart but compresses general merchandise margin mix.

headlines: It now costs $160 to fill up America’s top-selling vehicle as the Iran war nears its 10th week
